OVERRULED AS TO CAUSE OF ACTION 4. THE DEMURRER WAS PREMISED SOLELY ON THERE BEING NO CAUSE OF ACTION AGAINST AN EMPLOYEE AND THE PLAINTIFF'S FAILURE TO ALLEGE THAT THE INDIVIDUAL DEFENDANTS WERE HIS EMPLOYER. THE COURT CONCLUDES THERE CAN BE SUCH A CLAIM UNDER APPROPRIATE CIRCUMSTANCES. THE COURT DOES NOT REACH OR CONSIDER THE ADDITIONAL ARGUMENTS RAISED IN THE REPLY BRIEF CONCERNING THE ADEQUACY OF THE ALLEGATIONS ASSUMING THERE MAY BE SUCH A CAUSE OF ACTION. Notice Of Motion And Motion For Partial Judgment On The Pleadings
Off Calendar Aug-28-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. AVIVA N. ROLLER OF THE WORKMAN LAW FIRM, TEL. NO. 1-415-782-3660 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. JENNIFER G. REDMOND OF THE SHEPPARD MULLIN LAW FIRM, TEL. NO. 1-415-774-2910 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANTS KOKKARI LTD, GEORGE MARCUS AND KENNETH FRANGANDAKIS' Motion For Partial Judgment On The Pleadings is DENIED. Defendants' Motion for Judgment on the pleadings is improper because Plaintiff's Amended Complaint states facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action against Defendants. (Cal. Code Civ. Pro. 438(c)(1)(B).) Defendants allege that Plaintiff's remaining Fourth Cause of Action is barred because he did not exhaust his Private Attorney General Act ("PAGA") claims. (Labor Code 2698 et seq.) with the Labor and Workforce Development Agency ("LWDA"). Plaintiff, however, has satisfied requirements for administrative exhaustion under Labor Code Section 2699.3 by providing to Defendant and to the LWDA written notice of his intent to seek penalties for Defendants' alleged Labor Code violations. Plaintiff in his notice alleged that defendants violated Labor Code Section 510 for failing to pay overtime wages and Section 512 for failing to provide meal breaks. Discovery requests further elucidated Plaintiff's theories as to these causes of actions. Plaintiff's written notice provided to LWDA and defendants sufficient information about the facts and theories of his claim so as to allow Defendant to devise an appropriate defense and to allow LWDA to make an informed decision as to whether to investigate Plaintiff's claim. Defendants further allege that this Court must dismiss plaintiff's claim for penalties under section Labor Code section 226 because Plaintiff cannot recover both under the PAGA as well as under section 226. The PAG Act, however provides that an aggrieved employee may collect penalties for those Labor Code sections that have a penalty provision and for those Labor Code sections enumerated in the PAGA. (Labor Code 2699(g): "Nothing in this part shall operate to limit an employees' right to pursue or recover other remedies available under state or federal law, either separately or concurrently with an action taken under this part.") Defendants seek a judgment on the pleadings as to Plaintiff's Fourth Cause of Action, to which this Court already overruled Defendants' demurrer. Defendants, furthermore, do not address plaintiff's claim that defendants are liable for penalties under Labor Code section 558, which is not enumerated in the PAGA. Not all statutory penalties are "civil penalties" subject to the exhaustion requirements of the PAGA. (Dunlap v. Superior Court (2006) 142 Cal. App. 4th 330, 339.) Order signed in open court.
